topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the lab hummed a monotonous tune, mirroring the drone in Elara’s skull. She leaned back in her worn chair, the plastic groaning in protest beneath her weight. Staring at the screen, she noticed the familiar cascade of lines of code—the exact same structure, the same commented-out sections she’d wrestled with for the last three weeks. A slow, heavy slump settled into her shoulders, pulling her focus inward. *This is ridiculous,* she thought, rubbing the bridge of her nose with a knuckle. Then, a flicker of something sharp and unsettling: a file name on the shared drive, identical to hers, timestamped minutes apart. Her jaw tightened, a low, involuntary sound escaping her throat.

[story 2]

Mark dragged a hand through his hair, the motion jerky and uncoordinated. The coffee in his mug was cold, the dregs bitter on his tongue. He kept glancing between his monitor and the adjacent workstation. The schematic spread before him—the intricate wiring diagram for the prototype—was unnervingly familiar. He traced a connection point with his finger, the movement sluggish. A wave of profound weariness washed over him, making the edges of the room seem to blur. When he finally looked up, he saw it: a progress log, meticulously detailed, bearing his own initials. He leaned closer, his breath catching in his chest, a strange, hollow ache blooming in his stomach.

[story 3]

A deep sigh escaped Clara, a sound that seemed to carry the weight of everything she’d been trying to assemble. Her eyelids felt impossibly heavy, each blink a deliberate effort. She stared at the physical mock-up on the workbench—the brushed aluminum casing, the precise curvature of the housing. It was flawless. Too flawless. As she reached for a set of calibration tools, her fingers brushed against a label tucked beneath the main assembly. The handwriting was hers. A sudden, dizzying sense of disorientation overtook her; the sheer volume of work felt crushing, pressing down on her chest until breathing became shallow.

Staring at the screen, she noticed the familiar cascade of lines of code—the exact same structure, the same commented-out sections she’d wrestled with for the last three weeks. A slow, heavy slump settled into her shoulders, pulling her focus inward. *This is ridiculous,* she thought, rubbing the bridge of her nose with a knuckle. Then, a flicker of something sharp and unsettling: a file name on the shared drive, identical to hers, timestamped minutes apart. Her jaw tightened, a low, involuntary sound escaping her throat.

Mark dragged a hand through his hair, the motion jerky and uncoordinated. The coffee in his mug was cold, the dregs bitter on his tongue. He kept glancing between his monitor and the adjacent workstation. The schematic spread before him—the intricate wiring diagram for the prototype—was unnervingly familiar. He traced a connection point with his finger, the movement sluggish. A wave of profound weariness washed over him, making the edges of the room seem to blur. When he finally looked up, he saw it: a progress log, meticulously detailed, bearing his own initials. He leaned closer, his breath catching in his chest, a strange, hollow ache blooming in his stomach.

A deep sigh escaped Clara, a sound that seemed to carry the weight of everything she’d been trying to assemble. Her eyelids felt impossibly heavy, each blink a deliberate effort. She stared at the physical mock-up on the workbench—the brushed aluminum casing, the precise curvature of the housing. It was flawless. Too flawless. As she reached for a set of calibration tools, her fingers brushed against a label tucked beneath the main assembly. The handwriting was hers. A sudden, dizzying sense of disorientation overtook her; the sheer volume of work felt crushing, pressing down on her chest until breathing became shallow.
